era_context: The Early Bronze Age III in the Levant and Mesopotamia; the Akkadian Empire under Naram-Sin; Egypt's Old Kingdom in decline; the Indus Valley Civilization in its mature phase; early Bronze Age cultures across the Aegean and Anatolia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia beyond the Indus and early Chinese cultures

events.title: Naram-Sin's reign continues; consolidation of Akkadian control
events.description: Naram-Sin's reign continued during this year, with ongoing consolidation of Akkadian imperial control over Mesopotamia and neighboring regions. Administrative records and inscriptions from this period document the functioning of the Akkadian state apparatus, including tax collection, military organization, and trade networks. The empire was at its territorial and administrative peak, though internal tensions and external pressures were building.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Akkadian administrative centers

events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's reign is well-attested, but the specific events of individual years within his reign are rarely distinguishable in the historical record. The year -2401 falls within his reign, but no specific events for this exact year are clearly documented.

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization continues mature phase development
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization continued its mature phase development, with urban centers functioning as centers of trade, craft production, and administration. Trade networks extended across the Indian Ocean and into Central Asia. The civilization's standardized systems of weights, measures, and administration continued to function, supporting a complex urban economy.
events.certainty_note: The Indus Valley Civilization's existence and general chronology are well-established, but specific events for individual years are not documented.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the general chronology of this period remains subject to scholarly debate regarding absolute dating.
historiographic_note: The year 2401 BCE is not distinguished in the historical record from surrounding years. No specific events are documented for this particular year. The historical record for this period is sparse and relies heavily on archaeological evidence and inscriptions that do not provide year-by-year documentation. Most events described are general characterizations of the broader period.

graph_edges.note: Part of the same period of Akkadian imperial consolidation.
